chapters.chapter_summary: The patient was positioned supine with a shoulder roll elevating the right shoulder and head comfortably turned to the left, exposing the temporal parietal area. General anesthesia was administered.
A circular incision was marked on the scalp, and a circular bone flap was elevated using a single burr hole. The underlying relaxed dura was exposed.
The dura was opened, and the tumor was exposed and distinguished from the normal brain tissue. A fluorescent dye was used to enhance visibility, and the tumor was confirmed using computer navigation.
The tumor was carefully separated from surrounding tissues and removed using forceps. The area was inspected for any remaining tumor cells, and additional tumors were discovered and excised.

Hemostasis was achieved using a dilute hydrogen peroxide solution soaked cotton ball, which was applied to the surgical site. The area was covered with gauze pads after application of the antiseptic solution.

The bone flap was secured back into place using titanium microplates. Synthetic skin graft material covered the sutured incision line, and additional synthetic filler material was placed between the bone flap and surrounding cranial structure.

Postoperative MRI scans showed no radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. The pathology of the tumor was glioblastoma multiforme with MGMT promoter methylation and IDH1 or IDH2 mutation not detected. The patient had no neurological deficit and was discharged home.

operative_note.title: Temporal Parietal Craniotomy and Tumor Resection

SOAP.Subjective: I performed a temporal parietal craniotomy and tumor resection on a 20-plus year old patient who presented with several weeks history of headache, progressive weakness on the left side of the body, as well as difficulty with gait and balance. On physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, but had some left hemiparesis with upper tract signs.
SOAP.Objective: I identified a large mass at the temporoparietal junction with significant local mass effect and vasogenic edema. I observed the tumor being easily distinguished from the normal brain tissue upon opening of the dura. I used sodium fluorescein dye and computer navigation to confirm the location of the tumor.
SOAP.Assessment: I assessed the procedure as successful with the tumor seemingly removed in one piece in a gross total fashion. I identified and removed additional tumors near the initial resection site with no injury to the surrounding brain. Postoperative MRI scans showed no radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent tumor.
SOAP.Plan: I closed the wound in layers and secured the bone flap back into place using titanium microplates. I covered the sutured incision line with synthetic skin graft material and placed additional synthetic filler material between the bone flap and surrounding cranial structure. The patient had no neurological deficit and was discharged home.
